other_observers: Nathan Pieplow, Joey Kellner, Glenn Walbek, Steve Stachowiak, Bill Maynard, Jim Beatty, Larry Modesitt, Joe Roller; found at Lake Holbrook by Brandon Percival, Mark Peterson, Brad and Brian Steger, and seen there by Duane Nelson.
description: the bird was a large tern, significantly larger than nearby Black Terns, but smaller than nearby Franklin's and Ring-billed Gulls. The overall shape was typically tern like, with long, thin, pointed wings and long thin body. The wings were proportionally longer and thinner than those of Black Tern, giving the bird a more slender look. The tail was long and deeply forked. The bill was also long and thin, and completely black.  The entire upperparts of the bird were black or very, very dark brown. The bird had a black nape and crown; the entire top of the head was this color except for a white forehead, which was separated from the white underparts from a dark eyeline extending from the dark cap to the bill.  Then entire underparts of the bird were clean, bright white. The wing linings were also white, contrasting quite strongly with the dark brown/black flight feathers. The tail was the same dark brown/black color, with no white visible at the distances at which we were viewing the bird.Behaviors: For the entire observation the bird was flying over the water, usually quite high (almost always higher than Black Terns on the lake). The bird flew with deeper wingbeats than that of Black Tern, and with a more direct flight. Occasionally it would dive towards the surface, but was never observed plunging into the water.Call: none heardPlumage: adult alternate
